source_material.title: EEA Professional Climate Survey Report
source_material.body: Source material is the EEA Professional Climate Survey Report. It covers the European Economic Association / MinE professional climate survey of 861 respondents, focused on equality, diversity, inclusiveness, and comparisons with the AEA 2018 survey. The dry run should stay aligned to the report's general climate, opinions and perceptions, discrimination, avoidance, exclusion, and regional comparison structure.

expected_markers: actions taken to avoid possible harassment

expected_research_intake.research_context: EEA professional climate survey of current and former members, with Europe-wide regional comparisons and AEA 2018 context.
expected_research_intake.target_population_summary: Current and former EEA members and related economics professionals across Europe.
unresolved_gaps: The report publishes a respondent sample of 861, but it does not define a broader universe size for the underlying EEA population.
Small-country groups in the regional cut should stay under the minimum-base threshold.

objectives: Validate questionnaire wording against the EEA professional climate survey structure.
Check which closed-ended items should be charted, tabulated, or converted to evidence panels.
Surface segment suppression rules before human fieldwork.
Preserve AEA comparison context without overstating representativeness.
decision_questions: Which general climate items deserve topline charts?
Which regional or subgroup cuts need suppression or tabular handling?
Does the questionnaire separate climate, discrimination, avoidance, and harassment cleanly?
assumptions: Synthetic responses are exploratory scenario evidence only.
The 861-respondent report is not a representative census of the wider European economics profession.
AEA comparison text is contextual rather than a claim of full replication.
chart_plan: Render closed-ended toplines for general climate and discrimination items with explicit denominators.
Use tables for regional comparisons and underpowered subgroup cuts.
Replace open-ended follow-ups with coded evidence panels rather than raw text charts.

expected_question_rationales.rationale: Measures the headline general-climate satisfaction item that the report summarizes at 23% overall, 15% for women, and 31% for men.
Captures the discrimination and unfair-treatment block that the paper uses to explain the climate gap across groups.
Captures the structured avoidance/disclosure pattern that signals climate risk before the open-text follow-up.
Checks whether regional and country-group cuts preserve the Nordic, Italy, UK/Ireland, and Iberia patterns without overclaiming small bases.
Captures the single most important unresolved climate, discrimination, or fieldwork issue in free text for later coding.

expected_segmentation_behavior.minimum_base_rule: Suppress country-group or subgroup slices below the minimum base and state the reason explicitly.
expected_segmentation_behavior.suppression_rule: Use tables or warnings instead of unstable segment claims.

expected_report_warnings: Do not treat the 861-respondent report as a representative census of the wider European economics profession.
The report itself warns that response rates and representativeness are limited, so the synthetic panel must stay separate from the source sample.
AEA comparisons are contextual and should not be recast as a full replication claim.
expected_human_fieldwork_handoff: Use the dry run to validate climate, discrimination, and avoidance wording before human fieldwork.
Finalize country-group cuts and minimum-base rules before launch.
Proceed to fieldwork only after the questionnaire and disclosure language are locked.
